revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:宝寿院 (祇園社の社家)]]
[[File:祇園執行図.jpeg|thumb|During the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|qid=Q979873|lt=Gion Matsuri|1=Gion Matsuri}}, the Gion Shigyō (or Shigyō-dai) rides in a palanquin. The Shigyō was both a {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|qid=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} priest and a {{ill|Buddhist monk|ja|仏僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Buddhist monk|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 僧|1=Sangha|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q193944}}, appearing in monk attire.]]
'''Hōju-in''' (宝寿院) was a lineage of the {{ill|Gion Shrine Priesthood|ja|祇園社家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine Priesthood|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The Gion Shrine Priesthood was a hereditary class responsible for holding the high offices of {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社|1=Gion shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588442}} (now {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}}), including the {{ill|Shigyō|ja|執行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shigyō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 行政行為|1=administrative act|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1058236}}, {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|qid=Q2900274|lt=Bettō|1=Bettō}}, and {{ill|Chōri|ja|長吏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōri|1=chouri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11651573}}. The priestly line claimed descent from {{ill|Takenouchi no Sukune|en|Takenouchi no Sukune|qid=Q1073668|lt=Takenouchi no Sukune|1=Takenouchi no Sukune}}, a great-grandson of {{ill|Emperor Kōgen|en|Emperor Kōgen|qid=Q333572|lt=Kōgen Tennō|1=Emperor Kōgen}}, identifying themselves as an imperial branch of the {{ill|Ki clan|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. By at least the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, they had firmly established themselves as the hereditary Shigyō house of Gion Shrine.
Over time, the Shigyō lineage split into multiple branches, but during the early {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|qid=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, the family was unified under the house bearing the temple name "Hōju-in" (宝寿院). This system continued through the medieval and early modern periods until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}. In the early modern era, Hōju-in produced branch families such as Hōkō-in (宝光院) and Shinpuku-in (神福院), collectively known as the "Three Gion Monasteries" (祇園三院)<ref>{{cite book|和書|author=荒金喜義|title=京都史話|year=1966|publisher=創元社|doi= 10.11501/2988997}}</ref>.
The documents inherited by the Shigyō family include many records issued by shoguns of the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashikaga shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1154406}}, such as {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|qid=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, as well as imperial edicts. These records, known as the {{ill|Yasaka Shrine Documents|ja|八坂神社文書|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka Shrine Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, are designated as an Important Cultural Property<ref>{{Cite web|和書|website=文化庁データベース|url=https://bunka.nii.ac.jp/db/heritages/detail/134447|title= 八坂神社文書（二千二百五通）|accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>. The Shigyō family not only received donations from the Imperial Court, aristocrats, and shogunal households, but also gained revenue from operating lodging houses for pilgrims. Additionally, they held considerable influence as the de facto organizers of the {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|qid=Q979873|lt=Gion Matsuri|1=Gion Matsuri}}.
== Overview == === From the Shrine’s Founding to the Heian Period (Yasaka Clan) === The origins of {{ill|Gion Shrine|ja|祇園社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shrine|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園神社|1=Gion shrines|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11588442}} are not entirely clear. However, it is believed that during the reign of {{ill|Empress Kōgyoku|en|Empress Kōgyoku|qid=Q235296|lt=Saimei Tennō|1=Empress Kōgyoku}}, a diplomat from {{ill|Goguryeo|en|Goguryeo|qid=Q28370|lt=Goguryeo|1=Goguryeo}} was granted land in the {{ill|Yasaka-go|ja|八坂郷|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka-go|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 祇園|1=Gion|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q926312}} region (modern {{ill|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|en|Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|qid=Q1203862|lt=Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto|1=Higashiyama-ku, Kyoto}}). There, he established the {{ill|Yasaka clan|ja|八坂造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yasaka clan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and enshrined an ancestral deity from the Korean Peninsula. Over time, this practice merged with local Buddhist temples, evolving into a syncretic religious institution that ultimately enshrined deities such as {{ill|Gozu Tennō|en|Gozu Tennō|qid=Q11570247|lt=Gozu Tennō|1=Gozu Tennō}}, {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, {{ill|Butō Tenjin|ja|武答天神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Butō Tenjin|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 武塔神|1=Mutō-shin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10854373}}, and {{ill|Bhaisajyaguru|en|Bhaisajyaguru|qid=Q854773|lt=Yakushi Nyorai Medicine Buddha|1=Bhaisajyaguru}}<ref>{{cite journal|和書|author=久保田収|title=祇園社の創祀について|journal=神道史研究|volume=10|issue=6|year=1962}}</ref>.
This Goguryeo envoy is identified with {{ill|Iriji|ja|伊利之|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iriji|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a figure mentioned in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}. His descendants continued the Yasaka Clan’s religious practices until {{ill|Ki Momotsugu|ja|紀百継|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki Momotsugu|1=Ki no Momotsugu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11605090}} became a son-in-law and integrated the Yasaka Clan into the {{ill|Ki clan|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}, adopting the Ki surname. Since then, the area became the ritual center for the Ki family, and this is the official explanation of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}}<ref>{{Cite web|和書|website=八坂神社ウェブサイト|title=八坂神社の歴史|url= https://www.yasaka-jinja.or.jp/about/history/ |accessdate=2022-12-30}}</ref>.
Thus, the Gion Shigyō lineage emerged as a fusion of the immigrant Yasaka Clan and the ancient aristocratic Ki clan. However, some scholars criticize the lack of early historical sources linking the Yasaka Clan directly to Gion Shrine. There is no definitive academic consensus on the Ki clan’s precise role in the establishment of Gion Shrine.
=== From the Heian Period to Early Muromachi Period === It was during the era of {{ill|Ki Gyōen|ja|紀行円|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki Gyōen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} in the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}} that the {{ill|Gion Shigyō|ja|祇園執行|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion Shigyō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} family clearly became part of the {{ill|Ki clan|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}. Gyōen was a descendant of {{ill|Ki no Haseo|en|Ki no Haseo|qid=Q118767|lt=Ki no Haseo|1=Ki no Haseo}} (or Ki Momotsugu), and he served as both a Shinto priest and a {{ill|Tiantai|en|Tiantai|qid=Q668188|lt=Tendai|1=Tiantai}} monk at {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|qid=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}. At that time, Gion Shrine was a {{ill|末寺|ja|末寺|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/末寺|1=matsuji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11519671}} (subordinate temple) of Enryaku-ji. Officially, the highest offices were those of {{ill|Bettō|en|Bettō|qid=Q2900274|lt=Bettō|1=Bettō}} and {{ill|Chōri|ja|長吏|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chōri|1=chouri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11651573}}, with the Gion Bettō position held by the {{ill|Tendai Zasu|ja|天台座主|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tendai Zasu|1=Tendai-zasu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11442374}} (chief abbot of Tendai Buddhism). However, in practice, the Shigyō family gradually assumed de facto leadership of Gion Shrine<ref>{{cite journal|和書|author=野地秀俊|title=社僧再考 - 中世祇園社における門閥形成|journal=佛教大學大學院紀要|year=1998|issue=26|pages=1–15|url= https://archives.bukkyo-u.ac.jp/rp-contents/DO/0026/DO00260R001.pdf}}</ref>.
From Gyōen onward, the Ki clan hereditary lineage continued to hold the position of Shigyō at Gion Shrine up until the {{ill|Meiji era|en|Meiji era|qid=Q3125096|lt=Meiji era|1=Meiji era}}<ref>{{cite journal|和書|author=小杉達|title=祇園社の社僧|journal=神道史研究|year=1970|volume=18|issue=2|pages=33–48}}</ref>. Despite being a {{ill|Shinbutsu-shūgō|en|Shinbutsu-shūgō|qid=Q2279088|lt=Shinbutsu-shūgō|1=Shinbutsu-shūgō}} shrine that was listed among the {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|qid=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}}, its priests were monks who had taken Buddhist tonsure. These monks were referred to as {{ill|Shasō|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shasō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=Bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11396755}}. The Ki clan's priestly house, beginning with Gyōen, continued the practice of hereditary succession within the same bloodline, while also observing Buddhist tonsure.
Notably, even though they were Buddhist monks, they married and passed down the priestly role within their family, an approach that predated the similar practice later seen in {{ill|Shinran|en|Shinran|qid=Q512934|lt=Shinran|1=Shinran}}’s lineage. This was considered acceptable because they were also Shinto priests, and Gion Shrine was officially a Shinto institution. Some scholars argue that this practice was also a means for the Ki clan to maintain its power as it was declining in influence at the imperial court due to the rising dominance of the {{ill|Fujiwara clan|en|Fujiwara clan|qid=Q641628|lt=Fujiwara clan|1=Fujiwara clan}}. Formally, the hereditary succession took the shape of a **師資相続** (shishi sōzoku), meaning "master-disciple succession," in which actual sons or close relatives were nominally adopted as disciples<ref>{{cite journal|和書|author=小杉達|title=祇園社の御師|journal=神道史研究|year=1971|volume=19|issue=1|pages=15–30}}</ref>.
=== The Nanboku-chō Conflict and the Establishment of the Hōju-in Family === Over time, multiple branches of the Ki clan took turns holding the Shigyō position. The most powerful of these were two lineages: one that used the character 晴 ("Haru") in their names, said to be descended from {{ill|Abe no Seimei|en|Abe no Seimei|qid=Q1150151|lt=Abe no Seimei|1=Abe no Seimei}}, and another that used the character 顕 ("Ken"), descended from {{ill|Ki Ken'gen|ja|紀顕玄|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki Ken'gen|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. During the {{ill|Kamakura period|en|Kamakura period|qid=Q236205|lt=Kamakura period|1=Kamakura period}}, these two families alternated holding the titles of Chōri and Shigyō.
During the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, the Gion Shrine split into factions aligned with either the Northern Court or the Southern Court. {{ill|Ki Kensan|ja|紀顕詮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki Kensan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who supported {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|qid=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}} and the Northern Court, clashed with {{ill|Ki Seisei|ja|紀静晴|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki Seisei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who sided with the Southern Court. After the Northern Court’s victory, the Shigyō position was consolidated under Kensan’s lineage, and the Haru branch was eliminated.
Kensan’s son, {{ill|Ki Kenzan|ja|紀顕深|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki Kenzan|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who served as the personal priest ({{ill|御師|ja|御師|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/御師|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Onshi|qid=Q11488972}}) of {{ill|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|en|Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|qid=Q248381|lt=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu|1=Ashikaga Yoshimitsu}}, received an imperial decree from {{ill|Emperor Go-Komatsu|en|Emperor Go-Komatsu|qid=Q123052|lt=Emperor Go-Komatsu|1=Emperor Go-Komatsu}} recognizing his family's hereditary right to the Shigyō office. He was also granted the Rank of {{ill|殿上人|ja|殿上人|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/殿上人|1=court nobles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10369708}} (court nobility). Kenzan was the first to take the temple name **Hōju-in** (宝寿院), establishing the Hōju-in family as the hereditary rulers of Gion Shrine.
Additionally, Yoshimitsu’s decree confirmed that the traditional lands of Gion Shrine—spanning "from Sanjō in the north, to Gojō in the south, from Shijō embankment in the west, to Higashiyama in the east"—would be inherited by the Hōju-in family. Yoshimitsu’s policies also led to Gion Shrine becoming independent from {{ill|Enryaku-ji|en|Enryaku-ji|qid=Q917195|lt=Enryaku-ji|1=Enryaku-ji}}. From this point forward, the Hōju-in family became the hereditary priests of Gion Shrine and served as the **御師** (personal priests) to the shogunal Ashikaga family until the fall of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1154406}}<ref>{{cite journal|和書|author=花田卓司|title=南北朝期における将軍家御師職の意義|url=https://www.ritsumei.ac.jp/acd/cg/lt/rb/637/637PDF/hanada.pdf| journal=立命館文学|volume=637 |pages=1228-1245|year=2014}}</ref>.
== Early Modern Period and Beyond == After the fall of the {{ill|Muromachi shogunate|ja|Ashikaga Shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate|2=ja|3=室町幕府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi shogunate|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1154406}}, Gion Shrine continued to receive protection from the ruling authorities. This included a donation of 10,000 koku from {{ill|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|en|Toyotomi Hideyoshi|qid=Q187550|lt=Toyotomi Hideyoshi|1=Toyotomi Hideyoshi}} and additional patronage from {{ill|Kita no Mandokoro|ja|北政所|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita no Mandokoro|1=kita no mandokoro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11401757}}.
During the late Edo period, the final **Shigyō**, {{ill|Hōju-in Sonpuku|ja|宝寿院尊福|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in Sonpuku|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (who later secularized and took the name {{ill|Tachibana Shigetsugu|ja|建内繁継|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tachibana Shigetsugu|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}), witnessed the implementation of the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu Bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} (Separation of Shinto and Buddhism) policy, which mandated that all shrine monks ({{ill|Shasō|ja|社僧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shasō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 別当寺|1=Bettō-ji|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11396755}}) be secularized<ref>{{cite book|和書|title=新編 八坂神社記録|editor=八坂神社文書編纂委員会|year=2016|publisher=臨川書店|isbn=978-4653043096}}</ref>.
By the late medieval period to the early modern period, two branch families of the {{ill|Hōju-in family|ja|宝寿院家|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōju-in family|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, known as {{ill|Hōkō-in|ja|宝光院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōkō-in|1=houkouin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11452337}} and {{ill|Shinpuku-in|ja|神福院|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinpuku-in|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, had emerged as deputies (Shigyō-dai). By the late Edo period, these were joined by five additional monk residences—{{ill|Take-bō|ja|竹坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Take-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Matsu-bō|ja|松坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Matsu-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tōbai-bō|ja|東梅坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tōbai-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Seibai-bō|ja|西梅坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Seibai-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and {{ill|Shin-bō|ja|新坊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shin-bō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}—collectively known as the "**Three Temples and Five Monastic Halls of Gion**" (祇園の三院五坊). However, under the Shinbutsu Bunri policy, all affiliated shasō were required to secularize, and these institutions were entirely dismantled<ref>{{cite journal|和書|author=高原美忠|title=嘉永以後の八坂神社|journal=神道史研究 |volume=10|issue=6}}</ref>.
== Miscellaneous == === Family Crest === [[File:Japanese Crest Go Ka.svg|thumb|The **Gokarakomon** crest]] The priestly **Ki clan** (紀氏), which presided over Gion Shrine, traditionally used the **Gokarakomon** (五瓜唐花) crest<ref>{{cite book|和書|author=辻合喜代太郎|title=続日本の家紋|year=1975|publisher=保育社|isbn=978-4586503452}}</ref>.
A common misconception, dating back to the Edo period, claims that the emblem of {{ill|Oda Nobunaga|en|Oda Nobunaga|qid=Q171411|lt=Oda Nobunaga|1=Oda Nobunaga}} influenced the adoption of the **Mokōkaramon** (木瓜唐花) as the official crest of {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}}. However, historical records confirm that the shrine had been using the design long before Nobunaga's era. Modern scholarship refutes this theory and instead traces the crest's origin to the **Ki clan**, the hereditary priests of Gion Shrine<ref>{{cite book|和書|author={{ill|Takahara Yoshitada|ja|高原美忠|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takahara Yoshitada|1=Yoshitada Takahara|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11668932}}|title=八坂神社|year=1972|publisher=学生社}}</ref>.
== Priestly Lineage of Tsushima Shrine == The priestly families of {{ill|Tsushima Gozu Tennō-sha|ja|津島牛頭天王社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsushima Gozu Tennō-sha|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 津島神社|1=Tsushima Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q705136}} (now known as {{ill|Tsushima Shrine|en|Tsushima Shrine|qid=Q705136|lt=Tsushima Shrine|1=Tsushima Shrine}}) in {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, including the **Himuro clan** ({{ill|Himuro family (Ki clan)|ja|氷室家 (紀氏)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Himuro family (Ki clan)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) and the **Horita clan** ({{ill|Hotta clan|en|Hotta clan|qid=Q751004|lt=Horita clan|1=Hotta clan}}), were branches of Gion Shrine’s **shrine family** ({{ill|Shake (social class)|en|Shake (social class)|qid=Q11588367|lt=Shrine family|1=Shake (social class)}}). These priestly lineages were established when members of the Gion clergy were dispatched to Tsushima, where they took up hereditary shrine leadership. Like the Ki clan, these priestly families continued to use the **Mokōmon** (木瓜紋) crest, which was common among the Ki lineage.
== Related Pages == * {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|qid=Q979873|lt=Gion Matsuri|1=Gion Matsuri}} * {{ill|Yasaka Shrine|en|Yasaka Shrine|qid=Q692714|lt=Yasaka Shrine|1=Yasaka Shrine}} * {{ill|Ki clan|ja|紀氏|1=Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|de|Ki (Klan)|uk|Рід Кі|ko|기씨 (황별)|fr|Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|3=紀伊国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ki clan|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii no Kuni no Miyatsuko|qq=}}
== References ==
